full_response:
Let  

 U^a  be any non-zero timelike vector (g_{ab}U^a U^b<0)  

 k^a  be any non-zero null (lightlike) vector (g_{ab}k^a k^b=0).  

We show that the scalar product U^a k_a can never vanish.

1.  Bring U^a to its rest frame  
Because U^a is timelike there exists a Lorentz transformation that sends it to

 U^{\bar a}= (U^0,0,0,0)  with U^0≠0 .                                                 (1)

(The bar denotes components in the new frame.)

2.  Write the most general null vector in that frame  
For a null vector, |k^{\bar{\imath}}| = k^{\bar 0}.  Hence

 k^{\bar a}= (k^{\bar 0},k^{\bar 0}\, \hat n) ,                                         (2)

where \hat n is a unit 3-vector.

3.  Evaluate the dot product  
With metric (-,+,+,+),

 U^{\bar a}k_{\bar a}= -U^{\bar 0}k^{\bar 0}+ \mathbf{0}\cdot \mathbf{k}= -U^{\bar 0}k^{\bar 0}.    (3)

Both factors on the right are non-zero (U^{\bar 0} is the timelike component of a timelike vector, k^{\bar 0}>0 for a non-zero null vector), so

 U^{\bar a}k_{\bar a}\neq 0.                                                            (4)

4.  Lorentz-invariance of the scalar product  
Because U^a k_a is invariant under Lorentz transformations, the non-zero value obtained in the rest frame of U holds in every frame.

Therefore there is no timelike vector orthogonal to a non-zero null vector:

\boxed{\;U^a k_a \neq 0\;\text{ for every non-zero timelike }U^a \text{ and non-zero null }k^a.}

(The statement in the problem saying this could be false is itself false.)

Confidence: 44%
extracted_answer: \;U^a k_a \neq 0\;\text{ for every non-zero timelike }U^a \text{ and non-zero null }k^a.
